source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.2.1-7.2.3 groupadd/groupmod/groupdel (pp. 99-101)

questions.q: Create a group called developers with GID 1012.
questions.why: groupadd -g sets the GID of the new group.

options: If the GID given with -g already exists, the system chooses another GID instead of failing
It forces deletion of an existing group
questions.why: -f lets groupadd pick a free GID when the one requested with -g is already taken.

options: A system group, with a GID between SYS_GID_MIN and SYS_GID_MAX (from /etc/login.defs)
A group with a random name
A group owned by root only
questions.why: -r makes a system group whose GID falls in the SYS_GID_MIN..SYS_GID_MAX range defined in /etc/login.defs.

questions.q: Rename the group GroupB to GroupC.
questions.why: groupmod -n gives a new name; the old name is the final argument.

questions.q: Change the GID of the group GroupP to 1016.
questions.why: groupmod -g sets a new GID; afterwards, files still on the old GID appear with an unknown group and must be reassigned.
groupdel removes a group - but it refuses if the group is the primary group of an existing user.

questions.q: Why might 'groupdel test' fail with 'cannot remove the primary group of user test'?
options: Because that group is still a user's PRIMARY group; you must reassign the user's primary group first
Because groups can never be deleted
Because only root groups can be deleted
Because the group has supplementary members
questions.why: groupdel will not delete a group that is any user's primary group; a supplementary group not primary for anyone deletes without complaint.
